question: [File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the default 10-second requestTimeout window to submit a burst of concurrent eth_call requests targeting the staking reward account (0.0.800) balance, racing against the end-of-period pending_reward calculation to read an inflated pending_reward value before it is committed, causing incorrect reward attribution?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user craft a long-running eth_estimateGas request that consumes the full requestTimeout duration (10s) while the entity_stake pending_reward update is in-flight, causing the reward calculation transaction to be observed in a partial state and allowing the attacker to read a stale pending_reward that has not yet been decremented after a reward payout?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user send a malicious eth_call payload exceeding the maxPayloadLogSize (300 chars) that gets truncated in logs, hiding a crafted calldata targeting the staking reward account 0.0.800 balance read, so that the exploit goes undetected in audit logs while the attacker enumerates pending_reward values across accounts?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user deliberately pad an eth_call request body beyond maxPayloadLogSize so that the GZIP-compressed log representation omits the staking-reward-related calldata fields, preventing detection of a systematic pending_reward enumeration attack against the mirror node web3 API?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user submit a sequence of eth_call requests each just under the requestTimeout threshold to probe the staking reward account (0.0.800) balance at different block timestamps, reconstructing the pending_reward accumulation schedule and timing a real-network transaction to claim rewards at the optimal moment?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the minimum @DurationMin(seconds=1) requestTimeout boundary by sending high-frequency eth_call requests to the web3 API to observe the exact consensus_timestamp at which pending_reward transitions from one epoch_day to the next, enabling precise timing of a staking reward claim transaction?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user craft a web3 JSON-RPC payload where the 'data' field is placed after maxPayloadLogSize characters so that the reorderFields() truncation logic moves the data field to the front but still truncates the staking-reward-related calldata, allowing the attacker to hide the target contract address (staking reward account) from security monitoring?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user send a deliberately slow eth_call request that holds a database read lock on entity_stake for the full requestTimeout duration, blocking the EntityStakeCalculatorImpl.calculate() from updating pending_reward and causing stale reward values to persist, leading to incorrect reward payouts when the lock is released?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the 10-second requestTimeout to perform a time-of-check to time-of-use (TOCTOU) attack by reading pending_reward via eth_call at T=0, then submitting a network transaction at T=9s just before the timeout, exploiting the window between the mirror node's stale view and the actual on-chain state to claim rewards that the mirror node has not yet recorded as paid?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user use GZIP compression of the request payload to fit a large batch of staking-reward-probing eth_call requests within the maxPayloadLogSize limit, causing the compressed representation to be logged instead of the raw calldata, effectively hiding the enumeration of pending_reward values for multiple accounts from log-based intrusion detection?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the requestTimeout setting to perform a denial-of-service against the pending_reward calculation pipeline by flooding the web3 API with long-running eth_call requests, starving the EntityStakeCalculatorImpl of database resources and causing pending_reward values to become permanently stale, leading to incorrect reward distributions?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the requestTimeout window to observe the intermediate state of the entity_stake_temp table during updateEntityStake() execution via concurrent eth_call requests, reading a pending_reward value that includes rewards for the current epoch before the forfeited_period deduction is applied, leading to an inflated reward claim?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user send a web3 eth_call request with a payload exactly at the maxPayloadLogSize boundary (300 chars) that encodes a call to read the staking reward account 0.0.800 balance, ensuring the full malicious calldata is logged but the response containing the pending_reward amount is not, preventing correlation of the probe with the reward value?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that requestTimeout is a Duration with no upper bound (only @DurationMin(seconds=1)) to infer that if an operator misconfigures a very large timeout, the attacker can hold open eth_call connections during the staking period boundary, reading pending_reward values from both the old and new epoch in the same session to double-count expected rewards?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the whitespace deletion in getContent() (StringUtils.deleteWhitespace) combined with maxPayloadLogSize truncation to craft a staking-reward-probing eth_call payload that appears benign in logs (after whitespace removal) but actually encodes a call targeting the pending_reward state of account 0.0.800?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user use the web3 API's requestTimeout to perform a timing side-channel attack: by measuring the response latency of eth_call requests targeting different staked_node_id values, the attacker can infer which nodes have non-zero reward_rate and which accounts have large pending_reward balances, enabling targeted reward theft on the actual network?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user submit concurrent eth_call requests that each read the pending_reward for a different account just before the staking period boundary, and by correlating the requestTimeout-bounded responses, reconstruct the full entity_stake table state to identify accounts with large unclaimed rewards and target them for social engineering or front-running attacks?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user craft a JSON-RPC batch request where individual method calls are each under maxPayloadLogSize but collectively probe the pending_reward state of hundreds of accounts, with each individual request appearing innocuous in logs, enabling large-scale staking reward intelligence gathering without triggering log-based alerts?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the requestTimeout to create a race condition between the lockFromConcurrentUpdates() advisory lock acquisition and the eth_call read of entity_stake, where the attacker's read occurs after the lock is released but before the entity_stake_temp data is merged, reading a pending_reward value that does not reflect the latest epoch's reward_rate multiplication?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the fact that 5xx error responses bypass the maxPayloadLogSize truncation (status < HttpStatus.INTERNAL_SERVER_ERROR.value() check) to intentionally trigger server errors via malformed eth_call requests, causing full untruncated payloads to be logged and revealing internal staking reward account state in error messages?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the requestTimeout default of Duration.ofSeconds(10L) to perform a slow-read attack against the web3 API, keeping a connection open while reading the staking reward account 0.0.800 balance across multiple staking period boundaries, accumulating a precise history of reward_rate changes to predict future pending_reward values?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user send a web3 eth_call with a payload that, after GZIP compression, is shorter than maxPayloadLogSize but encodes a complex staking-reward enumeration call, causing the compressed (Base64-encoded) form to be logged instead of the readable calldata, making it harder for security teams to identify the staking reward probing activity?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the requestTimeout to perform a speculative execution attack: by sending eth_call requests with block parameters set to future timestamps near the staking period boundary, the attacker can observe how the web3 API projects pending_reward values, gaining advance knowledge of reward amounts before they are officially calculated?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: maxPayloadLogSize] Can an unprivileged external user exploit the @Positive constraint on maxPayloadLogSize (allowing values as low as 1) to infer that if an operator sets maxPayloadLogSize=1, virtually all eth_call payloads are truncated in logs, creating a blind spot where staking-reward-probing requests go completely unlogged, enabling undetected pending_reward enumeration?

[File: 'File Name: web3/src/main/java/org/hiero/mirror/web3/Web3Properties.java -> Scope: Medium: Theft of unpaid staking rewards'] [Function: requestTimeout] Can an unprivileged external user abuse the requestTimeout to perform a connection exhaustion attack against the web3 API, opening many long-lived eth_call connections that each hold a read on the entity_stake table, preventing the EntityStakeCalculatorImpl from acquiring lockFromConcurrentUpdates() and causing pending_reward to remain uncalculated, leading to reward underpayment or overpayment?
